clouds_SA_0: I'm not sure if it was explained verbally, but the dual role of water was shown in the animation with sunlight (yellow arrows) and thermal radiation (red waves): Clouds reflect a lot of sunlight i.e. incoming shortwave radiation (up to 80 %). This has a cooling effect on earth's surface. But they also act like an insulating blanket for thermal radiation i.e. outgoing longwave radiation. This has a warming effect. It's especially prominent after sunny days with mild, cloudy nights vs. cold, clear nights. However, this video did not explain this kind of duality. It rather dealed with the positive feedback-loop (warming) in subtropical regions with decreased low-level clouds vs. negative feedback-loop (cooling) in mid-latitude regions with increased low-level clouds. It's not clear yet, which feedback-loop will have more impact on global temperatures, hence increasing the uncertainty of predictions.

Rising temperatures in higher latitudes can both increase and decrease carbon uptake due to complex interactions in ecosystems:

Increase in carbon uptake: Warmer temperatures may extend growing seasons, allowing plants to photosynthesize for longer periods. Additionally, previously frozen soils may thaw, releasing nutrients that promote plant growth.

Decrease in carbon uptake: On the flip side, warmer temperatures can also lead to stress on vegetation, drying out soils or causing permafrost to release stored carbon into the atmosphere. Increased microbial activity in warmer soils may accelerate the decomposition of organic matter, releasing more CO₂.
LSMs_SA_1: Improving carbon uptake predictions would enhance climate predictions by providing a clearer understanding of how ecosystems absorb or release CO₂, refining models of the carbon cycle and its impact on global warming.
